title: Will We Know if AI Takes Over? Q&A with Benjamin Boudreaux
gist: RAND researcher Benjamin Boudreaux, with mathematician Alvin Moon, presents a formal social-choice-theory model of how AI gradually erodes collective human agency through three mechanisms, and proposes 'agency audits' as a new, currently-missing class of AI model assessment that governments and companies could adopt alongside existing cyber and bio risk evaluations.

insights.claim: AI can erode human agency through three distinct mechanisms: disenfranchisement (humans automated out of decisions), AI enfranchisement (AIs becoming voting members of decision groups), and agenda control (AI silently shaping which options humans ever see).
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete taxonomy investors and policymakers can use to classify AI-deployment risk; agenda-control risk in particular maps onto recommendation/curation products where the harm is invisible by design.
insights.quote: The AI doesn't cast a vote itself, but it shapes what options the humans see. It would be practically invisible.
insights.locator: How do you see this happening?

insights.claim: Agency loss to AI is framed as gradual and pleasant rather than a dramatic single event, accumulating from thousands of individually-reasonable delegation decisions until no human is effectively deciding anything.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: Implies risk-monitoring must track trends/trajectories rather than wait for a trigger event, supporting demand for continuous AI-governance tooling.
insights.quote: thousands or millions of these very reasonable decisions add up to a situation in which no human is really deciding anything.
insights.locator: What are the warning signs that we might be heading toward that kind of tipping point?

insights.claim: Boudreaux and Moon built their model on social choice theory, adding AI agents to decision-making coalitions to mathematically track how the human-to-AI ratio in 'decisive coalitions' shifts over time.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: Establishes a formal, quantitative basis for agency-risk metrics, the kind of methodology that could underpin future regulatory or audit standards.
insights.quote: We built our model on social choice theory, which is the mathematics of how groups come together and make decisions.
insights.locator: your real contribution here was to model these trends mathematically

insights.claim: Proposed concrete leading indicators of agency erosion include how often humans override an AI recommendation, how much time/space humans have to decide, and the scope of an AI's decision-making authority (one task vs. many).
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: These override-rate and authority-scope metrics are buildable telemetry, a potential product surface for AI-governance and compliance vendors.
insights.quote: how often do humans override an AI recommendation? How much time and space do humans have to make decisions? What is the scope of an AI's decisionmaking authority

insights.claim: The model points to an irreversible end state because AIs in control would have to choose to return power, humans may have lost the skills to retake control, and removing embedded AIs from decision structures would be too costly and complex.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: The lock-in/dependency thesis argues for early intervention windows, strengthening the policy case for proactive AI-deployment governance over after-the-fact correction.
insights.quote: once AIs are embedded in all of these different decisionmaking structures, it would just be very costly and complex to remove them.

themes.theme: Agency audits as a new assessment class

insights.claim: Boudreaux argues current AI model evaluations cover cyberattack and biological-risk potential but have no method to assess impact on human agency, and RAND is developing 'agency audits' to fill that gap for governments, companies, and researchers.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: Flags an emerging category of AI model assessment beyond cyber/bio risk; an early signal of where AI-safety evaluation budgets and standards may expand next.
insights.quote: We haven't had any way to assess what happens to human agency when we deploy these models in different decisionmaking contexts. We're working now to develop what we call agency audits.
insights.locator: How can the government and other groups use your modeling to avoid a future like that?

insights.claim: Boudreaux treats the very smoothness and convenience of accepting an AI recommendation as a warning sign, and lays out his own ideas before querying a model to avoid anchoring on whatever it returns.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Boudreaux
insights.so_what: A usable heuristic for any knowledge worker integrating AI: friction-free acceptance is itself a risk signal worth designing against.
insights.quote: The smoother it feels to just go with a recommendation, the more I think it might be part of the problem.
insights.locator: Has this research changed how you personally use AI?
